chunk_summary: This section discusses glucose infusion rate (GIR) calculation in parenteral nutrition, emphasizing safety, carbohydrate titration targets, and monitoring to prevent complications like hyperglycemia and ventilator dependence.
learning_objectives: Understand the formula for calculating glucose infusion rate (GIR) in parenteral nutrition.
Identify safe GIR ranges for different pediatric age groups and clinical conditions.
Recognize the risks of excessive dextrose delivery and strategies to mitigate them.
Learn the importance of frequent glucose monitoring during GIR adjustments.
key_facts: GIR formula: GIR (mg/kg/min) = [dextrose concentration (mg/mL) × infusion rate (mL/hour)] ÷ [weight (kg) × 60].
1% dextrose = 10 mg/mL; 10% dextrose = 100 mg/mL; 12.5% dextrose = 125 mg/mL.
Example: 10% dextrose at 20 mL/hour in a 10 kg child gives GIR = 3.3 mg/kg/min.
Neonates often start at GIR ~4–6 mg/kg/min; older children in critical illness start at ~2–4 mg/kg/min.
Excess dextrose can cause hyperglycemia, lipogenesis, and increased CO2 production, worsening ventilator dependence.
Persistent hyperglycemia should be managed by reducing dextrose before considering insulin.
reference_quote: Glucose delivery in PN is safest when prescribed and monitored as glucose infusion rate (GIR), because the same dextrose percentage can be safe or dangerous depending on weight and rate.
